article_title: 100 Chrome Extensions Steal User Data, Create Backdoor
core_thesis: A coordinated campaign published 108 malicious Chrome extensions across five developer accounts, collectively installed over 20,000 times, designed to steal Google accounts via OAuth2, provide a universal backdoor, exfiltrate Telegram sessions, inject ads, and proxy traffic through attacker-controlled servers. The extensions appear legitimate and provide expected functionality while running malicious background code that communicates with shared C&C infrastructure.
detailed_summary: Socket, a cybersecurity firm, identified 108 malicious Chrome extensions published under five accounts: GameGen, InterAlt, SideGames, Rodeo Games, and Yana Project. These extensions share command-and-control (C&C) infrastructure, indicating a single coordinated campaign. Over 20,000 users installed them. Half (54) are designed to steal Google accounts via OAuth2: they acquire a Google OAuth2 Bearer token at login, fetch user info (email, name, profile picture), and send that data to a remote server. The token itself never leaves the browser. Another 45 extensions contain a universal backdoor that opens arbitrary URLs from the C&C in a new tab on browser start, with no restriction on the URL. The remaining extensions exfiltrate Telegram Web sessions (e.g., 'Telegram Multi-account' extension overwrites local storage to take over accounts), inject ads into YouTube and TikTok, inject content scripts into all pages, or proxy translation requests through an attacker server. The extensions provide their advertised functionality to avoid suspicion. Socket reported all extensions to Google, but they were not immediately removed from the Chrome Web Store.

key_data_points: 108 malicious Chrome extensions were identified, with over 20,000 total installations (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates scale of supply-chain attack via browser extensions).

54 extensions steal Google accounts via OAuth2: they obtain a Bearer token, fetch user email/name/profile picture, and exfiltrate to a remote server (🟢 Red Teaming: OAuth token theft is a powerful persistence/credential access technique; can be used to hijack Google services).

45 extensions contain a universal backdoor that opens arbitrary URLs from C&C on browser start, with no URL restrictions (🟢 Red Teaming: this backdoor can be used for command execution, phishing, or further malware delivery; survives restarts).

Extensions provide expected functionality (games, translators, enhancers) to avoid suspicion while running malicious background code (🟢 Red Teaming: social engineering via functional decoy is a common TTP; useful for designing realistic phishing campaigns).

The 'Telegram Multi-account' extension steals active Telegram Web sessions by overwriting local storage with attacker data and force-reloading Telegram (🟢 Red Teaming: session hijacking of messaging apps can lead to lateral movement or data exfiltration).
Extensions share C&C infrastructure, indicating a single coordinated campaign (🟢 Red Teaming: shared infrastructure can be used to fingerprint and track threat actor campaigns).

Socket reported all extensions to Google, but they were not immediately removed (🟢 relevant to your interests: highlights slow response from platform vendors; consider this when assessing risk of browser extension attacks).
conclusions: The campaign demonstrates a sophisticated supply-chain attack via Chrome extensions, leveraging OAuth token theft and persistent backdoors to compromise user accounts and data. The use of functional decoys and shared C&C infrastructure indicates a well-resourced threat actor.

For Red Teaming, this article provides concrete examples of how browser extensions can be weaponized: OAuth token theft for credential access, universal backdoors for persistence, and session hijacking for lateral movement. These techniques can be adapted for offensive operations or used to train defenders.

The slow removal of extensions from the Chrome Web Store underscores the importance of proactive monitoring and endpoint detection for malicious extensions, rather than relying solely on platform security.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests as it details a real-world, large-scale browser extension attack that uses OAuth token theft, persistent backdoors, and session hijacking—all techniques you can study and potentially emulate in controlled environments. The technical specifics (e.g., how the OAuth token is obtained and exfiltrated, how the backdoor opens arbitrary URLs) provide actionable intelligence for offensive security exercises. Additionally, the article highlights the importance of monitoring browser extensions as an attack vector, which is valuable for both offensive and defensive roles. As a programmer, you can analyze the described code patterns to understand how malicious extensions blend in with legitimate functionality.
extra_advice: Consider building a proof-of-concept Chrome extension that demonstrates OAuth token theft (in a lab environment) to understand the mechanics and detection opportunities. This will deepen your Red Teaming skills and help you develop countermeasures. Also, monitor the Chrome Web Store for similar patterns using automated scripts that check for extensions with shared C&C domains.
